chapterTitle: Chapter 0: How To Read This Book
title: How to use the system book
summary: A quick orientation to reading the Tutor architecture book as a practical map instead of a code dump.
transcript: Start here like you would open a map before a long drive. This book is not asking you to memorize every file. It is giving you a feel for the system: what the learner sees, where the tutor gets context, how local memory is saved, and which areas need extra care. Read it straight through when you are new. Come back chapter by chapter when you are debugging. And keep one distinction in your head as you go: this book explains the product architecture, while Graphify is still the code architecture map. The best rhythm is simple. First understand the learning experience. Then use Graphify when you need exact code paths, dependencies, and blast radius.

title: The product shape
summary: A lively overview of Tutor as a document study workspace, AI tutor, memory system, and review surface.
transcript: The Product Map chapter is the wide shot. Tutor is not just a PDF viewer, and it is not just a chat box. It is a full study workspace, which means the important actions should feel connected: read something, ask a question, save the useful part, review it later, and inspect what the system did. Study is where the source material lives. Chat is where the tutor responds. The Library keeps learning material reusable. Revision turns ideas into recall. Admin gives the builder a clear window into model runs, tool calls, memory writes, and diagnostics. That is the shape the rest of the architecture protects. Every technical choice should make that loop calmer, clearer, and more trustworthy.

title: What the tools actually do
summary: A friendly translation of React, Vite, Dexie, Express, models, voice, search, PDF, and Graphify.
transcript: This chapter turns the tool list into plain language. React draws the interface. Vite keeps development fast. Zustand holds live UI state, like the current view, selected document, voice settings, and active book. Dexie stores the local learner notebook in the browser. Express handles the API routes that need a server boundary. Model providers answer, vision reads images or pages, search providers bring in current outside facts, and Graphify helps maintainers navigate the code architecture before they open a pile of files. The useful mental model is not a stack of impressive names. It is a relay team. Each tool owns a clear handoff, and the learner should feel the result as one steady study experience.

title: How the screen changes
summary: A short guide to App, navigation, lazy views, Zustand state, and context moving across screens.
transcript: Frontend Flow explains how the app changes shape without losing the learner. App reads the active view from Zustand, then renders Study, Analytics, Revision, Admin, or another focused surface. Heavy views load lazily, so the first workspace does not pay for everything at once. The store is the quiet handoff layer. It carries selected text, active document details, API key state, usage, voice settings, and active book context between screens. That is why a PDF quote can become a chat question, then a saved note, then a review card, without asking the learner to repeat themselves. The engineering goal is not clever navigation. It is continuity. The learner should feel like the app remembers the thread.

title: From document to study context
summary: A guide to uploads, PDF/image classification, extraction, and local document grounding.
transcript: Study and ingestion is where raw material becomes usable learning context. A learner uploads a PDF or image. The app works out what kind of document it is. Then extraction turns pages into text, images, and anchors the tutor can actually reference. That matters because grounding begins before the model speaks. If the learner asks about the current document, the local source should lead the answer. If the source is weak, missing, or ambiguous, the UI should make that visible instead of pretending. Ingestion is not just file handling. It is the first trust step before the tutor explains, quotes, summarizes, generates flashcards, or saves review material into the learner brain.

title: The live tutor loop
summary: A focused explanation of streaming chat, source-first context, tool calls, web search, flashcards, and voice.
transcript: Chat is the live tutor loop. It gathers the learner message, selected text, local document context, settings, and sometimes memory context, then streams a response from the server. Tools can help, but they should not feel mysterious. A web search is for current outside facts. Vision is for pages or images. Flashcard generation creates review material. Source cards and learning notes need provenance. Voice adds immediacy, but still needs clear controls. The chapter's main lesson is control. Local-source questions should stay grounded locally. Tool results should be visible. Generated study objects should be useful right away, but reviewable before they become trusted learner-brain evidence. The tutor can feel fluid without becoming opaque.

chapterTitle: Chapter 6: Memory, Dexie, And The Library
title: The local learner notebook
summary: A guide to Dexie tables, learning books, entries, evidence, artifacts, corrections, and why chat is not the memory.
transcript: Memory, Dexie, and the Library is the learner notebook chapter. A chat transcript can disappear, but useful learning state should survive as local records. Dexie stores books, entries, concepts, flashcards, evidence, model runs, tool jobs, retrieval events, artifacts, citations, and corrections. The MemoryOrchestrator is the bridge from a live conversation into durable study material. That bridge needs discipline. A generated note may be helpful. A flashcard may be ready to review. But evidence, mastery changes, and corrections need stronger rows with source, reason, and inspection paths. The Library is not a scrapbook of model output. It is the place where useful material becomes organized enough to revisit.

title: Review becomes reusable
summary: A guide to built-in books, generated learning books, flashcards, and stored chapter audio guides.
transcript: Revision is where learning stops being trapped in a chat transcript. Built-in books explain the system. Generated learning books save what the learner studied. Flashcards turn ideas into active recall. Chapter audio guides add a listening path, but they should not be live read-aloud every time someone presses play. They should be authored, stored, and calm enough to follow. The purpose is practical. Come back later, open the Library, and recover the topic through chapters, concepts, cards, and short explanations that sound like a guide, not a robot reading a manual. Revision is the slower half of the tutor. It gives the learner a place to return.

title: Seeing the system work
summary: A guide to Analytics as learner progress and Admin as local beta observability.
transcript: Analytics and Admin answer different questions, and keeping them separate makes the app easier to trust. Analytics asks, how is the learner doing? It reads local progress, mastery movement, confidence, interaction patterns, and study activity. Admin asks, what did the system just do? It shows model runs, tool jobs, retrieval rows, memory events, evidence, artifacts, citations, corrections, tuning settings, and diagnostics. One surface is about learning progress. The other is about system behavior. Together they keep the app honest. A learner should not need Admin to study well, but the builder needs Admin to see when a model call failed, a tool overreached, or a memory write deserves review.

title: One learner turn, end to end
summary: A story-style walkthrough of upload, ask, retrieve, answer, save, and inspect.
transcript: This chapter follows one learner turn from the inside. A document is uploaded. Study extracts context. The learner asks for help. Chat streams an explanation. A tool may retrieve a local passage, search the web for current facts, read an image, or generate an artifact. Memory may save a useful note, flashcard, citation, or evidence row. Admin records the model run, tool job, retrieval event, memory event, and any diagnostic signal worth inspecting. The example makes the background concrete. A strong tutor should explain the lesson. A trustworthy learning system should also be able to explain how that lesson was produced, which sources shaped it, and what changed afterward.

title: Which model does what
summary: A map of tutor chat, fallbacks, learning-book updates, vision, web search, voice, stored guides, and pricing.
transcript: The model map keeps provider behavior understandable. Chat usually goes through the configured text model path. Learning-book updates, trace explanations, vision, search summaries, and voice each have different jobs. That matters because a model call is never just magic. It has a purpose, a cost, a latency profile, and a failure mode. Web search is for current outside facts, not for replacing a learner's uploaded document. Live voice needs fast turns and graceful fallback. Stored chapter guides are different again: they are generated ahead of time, saved as local MP3s, and played back without another model call. The map helps the team ask a practical question every time: why is this provider involved right now?

title: Navigation and safety rails
summary: A guide to Graphify-first code navigation, debug surfaces, and safety boundaries.
transcript: Graphify, debugging, and safety is the maintainer's guardrail chapter. Graphify is the repository architecture graph. It is not the learner brain, and that distinction really matters. Agents should use Graphify to route changes before reading half the repository. Debugging surfaces show what the app actually recorded. Safety boundaries describe what should never happen silently: hidden model internals, ungrounded mastery changes, destructive corrections, confusing generated artifacts, or cloud assumptions before beta testing. The theme is disciplined visibility. Move quickly when the code path is small. Slow down when shared state, server contracts, or learner memory are involved. Either way, leave a trail the team can inspect.

title: Where to be careful
summary: A short guide to high-risk files, local verification, browser QA, and preserving product boundaries.
transcript: Maintenance Boundaries tells you where to slow down. Content books and copy are usually easy to change. Dexie schema, server routes, SSE shapes, websocket paths, Zustand fields, chat streaming, document ingestion, and generated Graphify artifacts are more sensitive. The chapter is not trying to make the project feel fragile. It is teaching proportional verification. Small copy change? Keep the check focused. Shared state or API behavior? Run broader tests. Visual change? Open the real app in the browser. Memory or evidence behavior? Add a regression test and inspect Admin. Good maintenance is not ceremony. It is attention with evidence, especially around the places where a quiet bug could mislead the learner.

title: Quick tour of the learner brain
summary: A lively guide to the foreground tutor, local learner-brain ledger, and background worker contract.
transcript: Here is the whole shape. LearningAI is trying to feel like one attentive tutor, but it has to keep its promises in an inspectable way. On the surface, the learner gets explanations, questions, document help, voice, review material, and a Library they can return to. Underneath that, the local learner brain records evidence, artifacts, citations, corrections, retrieval traces, model runs, tool jobs, and mastery movement. The important contract is between those layers. The live tutor can adapt quickly. It can notice confusion, change examples, and keep the lesson moving. Durable memory moves more carefully. If something becomes part of the learner brain, it should have a reason, a source, and a way to be inspected or corrected.

title: The ledger is the trust layer
summary: A guide to durable learner state, typed records, sources, and correction paths.
transcript: This chapter is the trust layer. A good tutor can be warm and flexible in the moment. Permanent learner state needs stricter rules. Every durable row should answer four questions: what changed, why did it change, what supports it, and how can someone inspect or correct it? That is why generated notes, flashcards, source cards, audio guides, model runs, tool jobs, citations, and corrections are explicit records instead of loose feelings. Some artifacts can be useful before they are fully verified. That is fine, as long as the status is visible. The app should never confuse a helpful generated object with proof. The ledger is where ambition becomes accountable.

title: Fast teaching, careful memory
summary: A walkthrough of live tutoring adaptation versus evidence-gated memory updates.
transcript: The teaching loop is where the app should feel most human. It can explain, notice when the learner is struggling, switch examples, ask a quick check, slow down, or schedule recall. Those signals should shape the next tutor move immediately, because responsiveness is part of good teaching. Durable memory is different. If the system wants to change mastery, confidence, or long-term learner state, it needs an auditable evidence row. That row should connect the change to an observed action, a source, or a review event. So the lesson can move quickly, but the learner-brain ledger moves carefully. That balance is the heart of the architecture: lively teaching, disciplined memory.

title: Grounding is the main boundary
summary: An energetic guide to local-source-first retrieval, generated artifacts, and visible citation states.
transcript: Retrieval and citations are the honesty boundary. If the learner asks about a page, a selection, an uploaded document, or a saved learning book, local source material should come first. Web search is for current outside facts or explicit requests, not for replacing the learner's own material. Generated artifacts and citations need visible states: ready, not checked, verified, conflicting, or unavailable. That may sound fussy, but it protects the experience. A learner can still use a draft note, a flashcard, or an audio guide, while the app stays honest about how much trust has been earned. The goal is not to make every answer heavy. It is to make hidden uncertainty visible at the right moments.

title: Admin is the operating room
summary: A clear guide to model, tool, memory, retrieval, evidence, artifact, correction, and tuning surfaces.
transcript: Admin is the operating room for local beta. It should answer practical questions without making the user dig through logs. What happened during this tutor request? Which model ran, which model failed, and why? Which tools were called? Which retrieval, memory, evidence, artifact, citation, or correction rows changed? Which settings shaped the run? Runtime tuning is the companion to that visibility. The user gets local controls for source versus web behavior, memory context, tool budget, diagnostics, and refresh cadence. Admin should be useful and humble. It reports recorded state. It should not pretend to know what was never captured. That honesty is what makes tuning possible.

title: Voice should respect timing
summary: A natural explanation of stored chapter guides, live voice boundaries, and playback controls.
transcript: Voice is powerful only when it respects timing. Live tutoring speech needs quick starts, quick stops, short turns, interruption, and honest fallback to text. Library chapters are different. They should not call a live read-aloud route every time someone presses play. The better pattern is an authored guide, generated once, stored as a local asset, and played with familiar controls: play, pause, progress, and speed. The guide should explain the chapter, not recite every paragraph. That is the important feeling. A learner should be able to listen while walking through the idea, with enough natural space for the structure to land. Audio is not a decoration. It is another study path.

title: Prove it locally before cloud
summary: A roadmap guide for proving learner-brain behavior before AWS or production sync work.
transcript: The roadmap is deliberately grounded. First, prove the learner-brain behavior in the browser. Evidence gates should work. Ledgers should be inspectable. Model runs, tool jobs, retrieval events, memory events, corrections, artifact provenance, diagnostics export, and stored audio should all be visible enough to tune. Only after that should cloud sync, tenant isolation, backups, production dashboards, and deployment scale enter the story. This is not anti-cloud. It is sequencing. The local app should earn trust before infrastructure grows around it. If the browser version cannot explain why a memory changed, a cloud version will only make that confusion larger and harder to debug.

title: Shared words keep the system honest
summary: A closing guide to precise vocabulary, trusted references, and source boundaries.
transcript: The glossary chapter is not decorative. Shared words keep a complicated learning system understandable. Evidence means an observed learner action, review event, source-linked event, or other recorded signal. A mastery delta is a recorded movement, not a mood. A correction is a visible review intent, not a silent rewrite. A citation has a state. An artifact has provenance. A learner brain is a local ledger, not a mysterious cloud memory. These definitions may feel small, but they do real work. When the team uses precise language, the product can stay ambitious without getting slippery. The glossary is how the architecture keeps its promises in ordinary words.

title: How the surfaces connect
summary: A guide to the reader's wireframe map and the relationship between Study, Chat, Library, Admin, and background systems.
transcript: Wireframe Connections is the design map. It shows how the learner moves through Study, Chat, Library, Admin, and the background systems that support them. The point is not decoration. It is orientation. A good interface should make the main routes feel obvious: read here, ask there, save this, review later, inspect system behavior when needed. Advanced surfaces like Admin and Graphify can exist, but they should not be confused with the learner brain. Admin observes the local app. Graphify maps the code architecture. The learner brain is the study ledger. This chapter keeps those surfaces visible and separate, so the product can feel powerful without feeling tangled.

title: Two moods, one product
summary: A guide to Cosmic Obsidian for active study and paper-style calm for Revision and documentation.
transcript: Theme System explains the app's two-mood design. Study, Chat, and core AI surfaces use Cosmic Obsidian: very dark surfaces, neon accents, glass, and motion. That is the active workspace, where the tutor feels alive and responsive. Revision and documentation become quieter, with paper color, serif type, and notebook texture. That is the return path, where the learner needs calm enough to read, remember, and review. The contrast is intentional. The app can feel futuristic without making every screen shout. The design system protects both energies: focused intensity for active learning, and analog steadiness for reflection. Good style here is not decoration. It is pacing.

title: Small components, real behavior
summary: A guide to rendered previews that make buttons, cards, panels, and controls easier to reason about.
transcript: UI Component Snapshots turn design rules into visible examples. Instead of describing every component in abstract language, this chapter shows compact rendered pieces: cards, controls, panels, charts, meters, and interaction states. The value is practical. When the app grows, snapshots help keep style decisions consistent. They also make regressions easier to notice, because a component that looked calm, readable, and aligned yesterday should still feel that way after today's code change. This is especially helpful in a product with two moods. A glassy AI control and a paper-style revision card can belong to the same app, as long as their behavior, spacing, and hierarchy feel deliberate.

title: Controls that explain the system
summary: A guide to local beta controls for tuning, diagnostics, source checks, correction overlays, and stored audio guides.
transcript: Local Beta Control Patterns is about trust through controls. Runtime settings, diagnostics exports, source checks, correction overlays, artifact provenance, citation states, local verifiers, and stored audio guides all need clear UI. The user should see what happened, what can be tuned, and what is still only local beta. That status matters. A button that runs a local check should say what it checked. A meter should reveal its source. A stored audio guide should play like normal media, without pretending to be live intelligence. This chapter is the product design version of the learner-brain rule: do useful things, but make the status visible. Control without clarity is just another hidden system.
